EE290 — Spring 2026

Optimizer Mismatch Drives Catastrophic Forgetting

Celine Tan · Katie Wang · Swetha Rajkumar · E Harrison — UC Berkeley
Abstract Setup MMLU Results Weight Analysis Causal Evidence Geometry C4 Loss Discussion
A major challenge in continual learning is catastrophic forgetting: fine-tuning degrades performance on previously learned tasks. We extend our prior vision-domain findings to large language models, showing that switching optimizers between pre-training and fine-tuning dramatically amplifies forgetting. We fine-tune Moonlight 7B (available in Adam-pretrained and Muon-pretrained variants) on a driving commentary task and measure MMLU accuracy as a proxy for retained pre-training knowledge. The most severe case — Muon-pretrained → AdamW fine-tuned — collapses MMLU from 59.8% to 23.0% (≈ random), while the matched Muon→Muon combination retains 58.9%. We provide direct causal evidence linking large RMS→RMS weight-update norms to forgetting, explain the asymmetry via implicit regularization theory, and show the effect persists across a C4 language-modeling task.

Experimental Setup

Models

Both checkpoints are Moonlight 7B MoE at step 42,000. The Adam-pretrained checkpoint (Moonlight_adam_hf_step_42000) and the Muon-pretrained checkpoint (Moonlight_hf_step_42000) have approximately the same MMLU accuracy (~59.8%) at the start, letting us cleanly isolate the effect of pre-training optimizer.

Fine-tuning: Driving Commentary (Celine’s runs)

LoRA fine-tuning on 50,000 driving-commentary examples, 2,000 steps. We evaluate two metrics at every 200-step checkpoint:

  • Driving sentence match — fine-tuning task performance on 5,000 held-out examples
  • MMLU accuracy — 5-shot multiple-choice on 1,995 questions (pre-training knowledge proxy)

We also compute weight-update norms for each run: Δ = θfinetuned − θpretrained, streamed over all 5,317 shared tensor keys (~16B parameters).

CombinationPre-trainFine-tuneMatch?MMLU dropRMS→RMS norm of Δ
adam→adamwAdamAdamW✓14.6 pp28.2
adam→muonAdamMuon✗2.4 pp2.9
muon→muonMuonMuon✓1.1 pp3.8
muon→adamwMuonAdamW✗36.8 pp32.9

Fine-tuning: C4 Language Modeling (Katie’s runs)

4 optimizer combinations × 3 random seeds, 2,000 steps, tracking C4 validation loss.

MMLU Forgetting Results

Figure 1. MMLU accuracy (%) over fine-tuning steps, annotated with the final RMS→RMS induced norm of the weight update Δ = Wft − Wpre. The norm value for each run is shown at the terminal point. Left: Adam-pretrained. Right: Muon-pretrained. Note that the highest-norm runs collapse fastest.

Figure 2. MMLU accuracy drop (pp) at step 2,000 for each optimizer combination. Hatched bars are mismatched pairs. The Muon-pretrained + AdamW fine-tuned combination (rightmost, 36.8 pp) catastrophically forgets nearly all pre-training knowledge.

−36.8 ppMMLU: Muon→AdamW
Near-random at 23%
−1.1 ppMMLU: Muon→Muon
Best retention (matched)
−2.4 ppMMLU: Adam→Muon
Mismatched but retains well

A key observation: driving accuracy is indistinguishable across all four combinations — all converge to ~96-97% sentence match by step 2,000 (see below). Without evaluating on a pre-training proxy like MMLU, the catastrophic forgetting is completely invisible.

Figure 3. Driving commentary sentence match (%) over fine-tuning steps. All optimizer combinations perform similarly on the task being fine-tuned — forgetting is silent.

What Specific Weights Are Changing?

Each norm measures the update vector Δ = θfinetuned − θpretrained, accumulated via streaming over all 5,317 shared tensor keys (embedding layers excluded) totaling ~16B parameters.

  • L∞ norm: maxj|Δj| — largest single weight change
  • L2 norm: ‖Δ‖₂ — total magnitude of change
  • RMS→RMS induced norm: max over 2-D weight matrices W of √(n/m)·σmax(ΔW), where σmax is the largest singular value (power iteration). This is the specific norm Muon minimizes during training.

Figure 4. All three weight-update norms for the four LoRA combinations at step 2,000. The RMS→RMS induced norm (right panel) shows the clearest separation: AdamW fine-tuning produces norms 7–9× larger than Muon fine-tuning, regardless of which pre-training optimizer was used.

Key finding: AdamW fine-tuning consistently produces large RMS→RMS updates (28–33) while Muon fine-tuning stays small (3–4). This is not a coincidence — Muon's update rule explicitly constrains the RMS→RMS induced norm. AdamW does not.

Causal Evidence: Norms Drive Forgetting

Figure 5. Scatter plot of weight-update norm vs. MMLU forgetting for the four LoRA combinations. Left: RMS→RMS induced norm (the one Muon explicitly constrains). Right: L∞ norm. In both cases, the two high-forgetting combinations (both AdamW fine-tuned) have substantially larger norms. The causal chain is: optimizer choice → norm of weight update → degree of forgetting.

Figure 6. RMS→RMS induced norm of ΔW for each combination, ordered by pre-training optimizer. Annotations connect the norm value to the MMLU forgetting outcome. Muon fine-tuning keeps this norm small in both pre-training settings; AdamW fine-tuning makes it large. For the Muon-pretrained model, this produces a 36.8 pp MMLU collapse.

The causal mechanism: during pre-training with Muon, weights are driven to a manifold where the RMS→RMS induced norms of gradient updates are small. This is Muon’s implicit regularization — it converges to solutions that minimize the RMS→RMS operator norm. When AdamW fine-tunes starting from this manifold, it makes large spectral moves (high σmax(ΔW)) that push the weights far from the Muon-pretrained manifold, overwriting the representations stored in the high-rank singular directions.

Weight Geometry: Directions, SVD Entropy, and PCA Justification

Figure 7. Left: Angle between the weight-update directions ΔAdamW and ΔMuon for each pre-training group (LoRA runs). Full-space angle ≈89° in the 16B-dimensional parameter space (this is expected by concentration of measure — random vectors in high dimensions are nearly orthogonal). The PCA-space angle, computed in the dominant subspace via dual PCA on the double-centred Gram matrix, is the interpretable quantity: 44.8° for Adam-pretrained and 58.6° for Muon-pretrained. These angles show that optimizer choice sends weights in meaningfully different directions. Right: Cumulative variance explained vs. PCA dimension k. The Muon group (m=6 runs) requires k=5 to reach 98.3% variance; the Adam group (m=4 runs) saturates at k=3. This directly justifies the choice of k=5.

Figure 8. Mean SVD entropy H(W) = −Σ pᵢ log pᵢ per transformer layer for the Muon-pretrained and Adam-pretrained base checkpoints, split by module (self-attention and MLP). The top-3 layers with the largest entropy gap |HMuon − HAdam| are annotated with their ΔH. Muon produces higher-entropy, higher-effective-rank weight matrices throughout; the gap is largest in the early-to-mid layers. These are the specific layers where optimizer mismatch is most likely to disrupt learned representations.

Figure 9. Overall SVD entropy by layer (both attention and MLP together). Muon-pretrained matrices are uniformly higher-entropy, meaning information is spread more evenly across singular directions. AdamW fine-tuning compresses this distribution, overwriting the high-rank structure Muon built.

C4 Language Modeling (Katie’s Runs)

Figure 10. C4 validation loss at step 2,000 (mean ± std, n=3 seeds per combination). Muon-pretrained checkpoints achieve lower loss overall. Within each pre-training group, matched optimizer combinations converge slightly better, though the gap is small since C4 is close to the pre-training distribution.

The C4 task is in-distribution for the pre-training data, so forgetting is less visible here. The matched-optimizer advantage is small but consistent in sign. The larger story is that Muon-pretrained models reach lower loss overall — consistent with the Moonshot AI findings that Muon produces better pre-trained representations.

Discussion

Why does Muon→AdamW forget so much more than Adam→AdamW?

Muon explicitly constrains the RMS→RMS induced norm during training, driving weights to a high-entropy, high-effective-rank manifold. AdamW fine-tuning ignores this constraint, producing large spectral updates (σmax(ΔW) ~ 33) that overwrite representations stored in the high-rank singular directions. Adam-pretrained weights already sit in a lower-rank region (lower SVD entropy), so AdamW fine-tuning causes a smaller relative disruption even though the norms are similar.

Why does Adam→Muon forget less than Adam→AdamW?

Muon fine-tuning constrains its own RMS→RMS norm (norm = 2.9 vs AdamW’s 28.2), producing small spectral updates that refine the Adam-pretrained representations rather than overwriting them. This is also consistent with the PCA angle: the Muon update direction is closer to the matched optimizer (44.8°) than the AdamW update direction for the same pre-training group.

Implications for practitioners

Warning: If you fine-tune a Muon-pretrained model with AdamW — the default in most libraries — you risk a 37 percentage-point MMLU collapse. This forgetting is completely invisible from the fine-tuning task performance curves alone.

Addressing reviewer feedback

  • Which specific weights are plotted: Δ = θft − θpre over all 5,317 shared parameter tensors (attention Q/K/V/O projections + MLP gate/up/down projections), ~16B scalars total. Embedding and norm layers excluded.
  • Causal evidence: Figures 5–6 directly show that high RMS→RMS norm of ΔW correlates with MMLU forgetting across all four combinations. The mechanism is that AdamW ignores Muon’s norm constraint and makes large spectral updates.
  • Why k=5 for PCA: Figure 7 (right) shows the cumulative variance curve — k=5 captures 98.3% of variance for the Muon group, and k=3 captures 100% for the Adam group (3 runs = at most 2 non-trivial components).
  • Specific layers to analyze: Figure 8 identifies the top-3 layers with the largest Muon vs Adam SVD entropy gap for attention and MLP separately. These are the layers where optimizer geometry differs most and where mismatch is most likely to disrupt learned representations.
